title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read an article about extreme sports and the psychology of risk-taking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: On the Edge
content.subtitle: An article exploring why people are drawn to extreme sports and the risks they involve.
content.text:
Every year, thousands of people choose to climb mountains, jump from aircraft, paddle whitewater rivers, and descend cliff faces — activities that carry a statistically measurable risk of serious injury or death. From the outside, this can seem difficult to comprehend. From the inside, participants describe experiences of clarity, focus, and aliveness that they struggle to find anywhere else. [31] Understanding why some people are drawn to extreme environments is not simply a matter of curiosity; it has implications for how we think about risk, reward, and what it means to live fully. [/31]

The psychological literature on risk-taking has moved on considerably from the early view that thrill-seekers are simply reckless or poorly calibrated to danger. Research now suggests that many who pursue extreme sports have, in fact, a more sophisticated relationship with risk than the average person. [32] They tend to invest heavily in training, equipment, and knowledge; they are acutely aware of their own limits; and they make decisions according to carefully developed frameworks that distinguish between acceptable and unacceptable hazard. [/32] The risk they take on is chosen, calculated, and, in their view, manageable — which is quite different from the kind of impulsive risk-taking associated with poor decision-making.

For many practitioners, extreme sports offer something that ordinary life rarely provides: a state of complete absorption in the present moment. Psychologists describe this as 'flow' — a condition of full engagement in which self-consciousness recedes and time seems to alter. [33] In a free fall or on a technical rock face, the mind cannot wander; the demands of the situation fill every unit of attention. This enforced presence is, for many, the point — and it explains why those who experience it often find that ordinary activities feel flat by comparison. [/33]

Critics of extreme sports raise legitimate concerns. The environmental impact of expeditions to remote mountain ranges, the cost of rescue operations funded by public money, and the effect of high-profile deaths on the families of those involved are all arguments for greater caution — or at least greater accountability. [34] Some practitioners have engaged seriously with these criticisms, developing codes of practice around environmental responsibility and self-sufficiency that aim to reduce both ecological damage and the burden on emergency services. [/34]

Gender has long been an interesting dimension of the extreme sports world. For much of its history, the field was dominated by men, and the culture reflected this — with risk-taking coded as masculine, and women who participated often described through the lens of their gender rather than their achievement. [35] This is changing. Women now compete and perform at the highest levels across a wide range of extreme disciplines, and researchers have noted that women's approaches to risk management in these contexts are often more methodical and less ego-driven than those of their male counterparts. [/35]

The appeal of extreme sports is unlikely to diminish. If anything, the increasingly managed and mediated nature of contemporary life — in which spontaneity is rare and genuine uncertainty rarer still — may make the pull of the edge stronger rather than weaker. [36] For those who seek it, the willingness to stand at the boundary of what is safe is not a failure of judgement but a form of inquiry: a way of asking, under conditions that demand an honest answer, what one is truly capable of. [/36]

questions.question: The author suggests that studying why people engage in extreme sports is important because
options: A) it could help reduce the number of fatalities in dangerous activities.
B) it offers broader insights into human attitudes towards risk and fulfilment.
C) it could help develop better equipment and safety procedures.
D) it explains why some people are more adventurous than others.
questions.explanation: Understanding the appeal 'has implications for how we think about risk, reward, and what it means to live fully' — broader insights beyond just the sports themselves.

questions.question: Research into extreme sports participants challenges the idea that they are reckless by showing that
options: A) most of them are professionals who are paid to take risks.
B) they are physically stronger and more resilient than ordinary people.
C) they approach risk in a careful and knowledgeable way.
D) they tend to stop participating after their first serious injury.
questions.explanation: Research shows they 'invest heavily in training, equipment, and knowledge', are 'acutely aware of their own limits', and use 'carefully developed frameworks' — all evidence of a careful approach.

questions.question: The author describes 'flow' as particularly appealing to extreme sports participants because
options: A) it produces a physical sensation of pleasure similar to exercise.
B) it forces the mind to be fully in the present, which is rare in daily life.
C) it allows participants to temporarily forget about personal problems.
D) it can only be achieved through activities that involve physical danger.
questions.explanation: 'The mind cannot wander' during extreme activities — 'this enforced presence is, for many, the point', and explains why ordinary activities feel 'flat' afterwards. It's the rarity of being fully present.

questions.question: The author's attitude towards critics of extreme sports is that their concerns
options: A) are exaggerated and fail to understand the culture of the extreme sports community.
B) are valid and have prompted responsible responses from some practitioners.
C) are mainly motivated by a desire to restrict individual freedom.
D) should lead to tighter government regulation of dangerous activities.
questions.explanation: The author describes the concerns as 'legitimate' and notes that 'some practitioners have engaged seriously with these criticisms', developing responsible codes of practice.

questions.question: Recent research into women in extreme sports has found that they
options: A) are more likely than men to be injured due to receiving less training.
B) participate mainly in less physically demanding disciplines.
C) often demonstrate a more careful approach to managing risk than male participants.
D) have faced resistance from governing bodies that limit their participation.
questions.explanation: Researchers have noted that women's risk management approaches 'are often more methodical and less ego-driven than those of their male counterparts'.

questions.question: In the final paragraph, the author presents extreme sports participation as
options: A) an addiction that is difficult to give up once experienced.
B) a response to the lack of genuine challenge in modern everyday life.
C) a fundamentally selfish activity that puts others at risk.
D) a sign of psychological instability in those who pursue it.
questions.explanation: The author links the appeal to 'the increasingly managed and mediated nature of contemporary life', suggesting it is a response to the absence of genuine uncertainty and spontaneity.
